# Transcrição: 02- 1.1 Homework for Life

- **Fonte:** 02- 1.1 Homework for Life
- **Duração:** 10:31
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:08]

Let's talk about homework for life. It is the most important thing that I teach anyone. So buckle up. It is the thing you have to do if you want to gather stories. When I started storytelling back in 2011, I made a list of all the stories that I thought it would tell over the course of time. And as I started taking stages first in New York and then Boston and then around the country, that list was quickly growing shorter. And I always wanted new stories to tell. I didn't want to be one of those storytellers who takes the stage every night and sort of tells the same story or the same dozen stories. They roll out the old chestnut, something everyone's heard before. I wanted to be the kind of person who had a brand new story to tell every time he took the stage. Because I'm an elementary school teacher and I had a problem, my instinct was to turn to homework. And so I gave myself this homework assignment back in 2013. I'm giving it to you now. I call it homework for life. It's homework for life because if you begin doing it and you begin doing it right, you'll do it till the day you die. It's very simple. I decided this for myself. At the end of every day, regardless of the day that I have just lived, I'm going to sit down and ask myself, what is the most story worthy moment that happened today? Which is to say, what took place that was slightly different or slightly interesting or new over the course of the day, even if that day contained nothing really interesting at all. The actual prompt I give myself is this. I imagine that someone kidnaps my wife, Alicia, and my two children. And they tell me, we will not give your family back until you take a stage and tell a story about something that happened today, even if nothing really happened. So I forced myself to find the most story worthy moment of the day, even if it's utterly unstoryworthy. And I write it down. Now I don't write the whole story down. That's crazy. Those people are called journalists. They tend to write a lot right after they're broken up with and love falls away. And when they find new love, they tend to take those journals and stuff them in a drawer and leave them alone until love leaves again. I like to create simple, repeatable processes that can change your life. And that's what homework for life is. And so what I did was I created a spreadsheet, two columns. As you can see here, the A column is the date and the B column stretched across the screen is the place where I write my story. And that's the only space I afford myself to record the story from the day. My goal was find one new good story per month, 12 new stories per

## [03:08 - 06:17]

year. And if that had happened, I would have been thrilled. But instead, something miraculous happened and not just for me. For tens of thousands of people all over the world, they've experienced the same thing. I discovered that my life is filled with stories. The tragedy of our lives is this. We live our lives as if the days don't matter. We toss them away like they're meaningless. If I was to ask you right now, what did you do last Thursday? You would probably fight to think of something that took place last Thursday. Let's play the worst game in the world. Take your age, whatever it is. I'm 52. Subtract 12. So for me, 52 minus 12 is 40. Whatever your number is, think about that year of your life. How many things do you remember happening in that year of your life? How many stories from that year can you tell? Unless you got married, got divorced, had a baby, maybe made a big move, started a new job, you probably can't remember a lot about a year, just a dozen years ago. And even if you can, even if you did have that marriage or that divorce or that job change, now you've reduced an entire year of your life to a single event. But that's not really what the year was like for you. 12 years ago, you went around the sun 365 days, and a multitude of things happened to you. People said things that caused your heart to soar and broke your heart. You witnessed things for the first time. You did things for the first time. You had thoughts filter through your mind that you had never thought before. You had feelings and hopes and dreams. And then like everyone else, you left them behind. You tossed them aside like they're meaningless, but they're not. Homework for life is the acknowledgement that our days are worth holding onto, that our days are filled with stories, that as you begin to look at your life through the lens of narrative and you ask yourself at the end of every day, what happened today? You'll start to see things that you right now cannot see. Homework for life is the acknowledgement that your days are worth remembering, that you are worth remembering. I hear people say all the time that time flies, and that is a stupid thing to say. It's not true. Ask someone in prison if time flies. Ask a soldier in a trench in Ukraine if time flies. It does not. The problem is we toss our time aside.

## [06:18 - 09:19]

We fail to hold on to our days. Time does not fly by. It slips through our fingers because we make no attempt to hold on to it. My children, while I'm recording this, are 14 years old and 10 years old, and they feel 14 and 10 and not in a negative way in the way that when I think about Clara's life, I feel the 14 years because thank God I've been doing homework for life for most of her life. I've been capturing the things that she says and the things that she does, and the same for Charlie. They feel their age to me because I've been taking hold of every day, and so many of my days are filled with their stories. Time does not fly by. Time ticks slowly if you hold on to it. If you acknowledge that your days are meaningful and that every single day something happens that's worth capturing and holding on to. Homework for life will develop a lens for narrative that will allow you to see your life in an entirely new way. And if I'm being honest, the way I do homework for life today is completely different than the way I began. When I began in 2013, I would sit down at the end of the day, reflect upon my day, and find something that I could write down, and I would, and it was great. Today, I see homework for life moments constantly. Thankfully, my computer is usually within my vicinity, so when I hear something or think something or see something or experience something, I'm at my computer and I'm quickly writing it down, but if I'm not at my computer, my phone is in my hand. I'm taking a note of it. I'm grabbing a sticky note and writing it and stuffing it in my pocket. I see my homework for life moments. I see the meaningful moments of my life so much more clearly now than I did 10 years ago. And again, I'm not a unicorn. I'm no different than you. Tens of thousands of people have experienced the same thing. I hear from them all the time telling me that homework for life has changed their lives. I've taught homework for life to my fifth graders, 10 year olds. Some of them are now 25 years old and I hear from them all the time. They say to me, I can't believe I have a memory of every single day of my seventh grade year. Can you remember anything from your seventh grade year? If you can, can you remember more things than you have fingers on your hand? Probably not. And yet that year, seventh grade, was probably filled with things that were worth remembering, but we tossed them aside.

## [09:20 - 10:31]

I want you to start homework for life because I want you to start seeing the things that I see. I want you to start doing homework for life because I want you to have more stories than you have time to tell, which is the problem I have today. I started homework for life, remember, because I was running out of stories to tell. My list right now of stories that I have yet to craft, yet to tell on a stage, sort of ideas for stories that I have yet to even address, that list is now 700 items long. I have become a person who has run out of time to tell all of his stories and that's a wonderful problem to have. It's a problem I want you to have. When you have a life filled with stories like I do now, your life feels fuller, feels more complete, feels richer, you will feel happier by collecting the days, holding on to them, and recognizing that you are worth being remembered, that your days are worth being remembered, and that your days are filled with stories.
